metadata.summary: Structured decision process for selecting the right AI approach (prompt, RAG, fine-tuning) and model tier. Progressive enhancement strategy reduces cost 40-60% through complexity-based routing.

content.title: ML/AI Decision Framework
content.summary: A structured decision process for selecting the right AI approach (prompt engineering, RAG, fine-tuning) and the right model tier for a given task. Applies a progressive enhancement strategy: start with prompting → add RAG when external data is needed → add fine-tuning only when behavioral specialization is required at scale. Implements complexity-based model routing to reduce cost by 40-60%.

why.p: Defaulting to GPT or Opus for all tasks leads to 2-3x overspending on simple tasks. Fine-tuning ROI is often overestimated: 6x inference cost + training + maintenance rarely breaks even below 1M requests/month. Model pricing shifts dramatically — the framework must be re-evaluated quarterly. A lightweight complexity classifier routing simple tasks to DeepSeek and complex tasks to Opus captures most of the savings with minimal complexity.

list.item: Starting a new AI feature and choosing between prompting, RAG, or fine-tuning
Monthly API spend has grown and you suspect model over-selection
A model is deprecated or a cheaper/faster alternative has appeared
Different pipeline stages require different capability/cost trade-offs
Evaluating whether to switch providers for a specific workload
The task is trivial and the model is already decided — don't over-engineer
A deadline is imminent — pick the safe default (Claude Sonnet or GPT-4o) and optimize later
Purely creative tasks (marketing copy, story generation) — model quality differences are subjective; user preference testing matters more than a framework

section.name: progressive-enhancement-strategy
section.summary: Stack enhancement strategy: start with prompt engineering, add RAG only when external data is needed, add fine-tuning only at scale.

rule.statement: Start with prompt engineering; add RAG only when external/private data is required; add fine-tuning only when behavioral specialization is required at scale (greater than 1M requests/month).
rule.rationale: Each step up the stack adds cost, complexity, and maintenance burden. Fine-tuning at low volume rarely breaks even: 6x inference cost + training cost + dataset maintenance outweighs quality gains for most workloads.

section.p: Decision path: prompt engineering ($0 extra, hours to deploy) → RAG ($70-1000/month infra, days to deploy) → fine-tuning (6x inference cost, weeks to deploy, ongoing dataset maintenance).

section.name: when-to-choose-rag
section.summary: RAG is appropriate when data changes frequently, is private/proprietary, requires citations, or exceeds context window.

rule.statement: Choose RAG when: data changes frequently (daily/weekly), knowledge is private/proprietary, users need source citations, or the corpus is too large for context window.
rule.rationale: RAG doesn't require retraining as data changes. It provides traceable citations. It scales to large corpora without context window limits.

antipattern.name: rag-on-small-corpus
antipattern.description: Using RAG when the corpus is under 50 documents that fit in a single long-context prompt.
antipattern.reason: RAG adds retrieval latency, chunking artifacts, and index maintenance overhead. For small static corpora, stuffing everything into a single prompt is simpler and more accurate.

section.name: when-to-choose-fine-tuning
section.summary: Fine-tuning is appropriate for behavioral specialization at high volume, not factual recall.

rule.statement: Choose fine-tuning only when: knowledge is behavioral (writing style, domain jargon, persona) rather than factual, data is stable (updates monthly at most), and volume is high enough for ROI.
rule.rationale: Fine-tuning bakes behavioral patterns into model weights. It doesn't help with factual recall (use RAG for that). At low volume, 6x inference cost rarely recovers the training investment.

antipattern.name: fine-tuning-roi
antipattern.description: Committing to fine-tuning without calculating ROI: training cost + 6x inference cost + dataset maintenance vs quality gain vs volume.
antipattern.reason: Fine-tuning decisions are hard to reverse short-term. The dataset must be maintained and retraining must happen as the base model updates.

section.name: hybrid-approach-raft
section.summary: Combine fine-tuned model with RAG when behavioral specialization and current facts both matter.
section.p: Combine fine-tuned model with RAG retrieval when: domain-specific language/terminology requires fine-tuning AND current factual information requires RAG. Use fine-tuning to teach the model how to reason about your domain; use RAG to provide current facts.

section.name: model-tier-routing
section.summary: Route by task complexity, not by default. Save 40-60% through intelligent model selection.

rule.statement: Route by task complexity, not by default: simple extraction/classification → DeepSeek V3 or Haiku; standard generation → Sonnet or GPT-4o; complex reasoning where errors cost money → Opus.
rule.rationale: Model routing saves 40-60% on typical mixed-workload pipelines. Using Opus for simple tasks is 50-100x more expensive than DeepSeek for the same quality on mechanical tasks.

section.p: 2025-2026 cost reference (per 1M tokens): DeepSeek V3 $0.14/$0.28 input/output; Claude Haiku 3.5 $0.80/$4.00; GPT-4o $2.50/$10.00; Claude Sonnet 4 $3.00/$15.00; Gemini 2 Pro $3.50/$10.50; Claude Opus 4.5 $15.00/$75.00. Re-evaluate quarterly — pricing shifts dramatically.

rule.statement: Measure actual task distribution before implementing routing; deploy with one model for 2 weeks, collect traces, then apply routing based on real workload data.
rule.rationale: Routing logic based on assumed task distribution may route the wrong way. Real traces reveal that "complex" tasks may be only 5% of volume, making the routing overhead not worth it.

section.name: latency-budgets
section.summary: Match model selection to latency constraints. Opus in synchronous flows breaks UX.

rule.statement: Do not use Opus in synchronous user-facing flows; 10s+ responses break user experience. Use async/batch for long-running Opus tasks.
rule.rationale: Opus generates at ~50 tokens/sec; a 500-token response takes ~10s. User-facing latency tolerance is 2-4s maximum for interactive chat.

section.p: Latency reference: GPT-4o/Sonnet ~120-180 tok/s (~2.8-4.2s for 500 tokens); Opus ~50 tok/s (~10s); DeepSeek V3 ~100 tok/s (~5s).

section.name: multi-model-routing
section.summary: Implementation rules for routing across multiple models in production pipelines.

rule.statement: Enforce structured output on every model in a multi-model routing pipeline; different models produce inconsistent output formats without enforcement.
rule.rationale: If one stage uses Opus and another uses Haiku, the downstream stage may break on format differences. Structured output (Pydantic schema) normalizes this across models.

rule.statement: Set a hard budget cap in Helicone/Langfuse to catch runaway agentic loops calling expensive models in retry loops.
rule.rationale: An agent that retries Opus calls in a loop can exhaust a monthly budget in minutes. Hard caps trigger alerts before costs become critical.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: An orchestrator agent receives a task description and routes it through the decision framework: it first checks whether external/private data is needed (→ RAG), then whether behavioral specialization is required (→ fine-tuning), and finally selects the appropriate model tier based on task complexity. For high-volume pipelines, a lightweight classifier subagent scores incoming tasks by complexity (simple/medium/complex) and routes them to DeepSeek, Sonnet, or Opus respectively, logging actual cost vs. quality to a metrics store for continuous optimization.

Recommended subagents: Complexity classifier subagent — lightweight Haiku/DeepSeek call that scores task complexity before routing to a heavier model. Cost monitor subagent — reads Langfuse/Helicone traces, computes per-task cost, flags when routing decisions are suboptimal.

section.name: prompt-pattern
section.summary: Task router prompt for structured decision output.
section.code:
You are a task router. Given the following task description, output JSON: { "approach": "prompt_engineering" | "rag" | "fine_tuning", "model": "haiku" | "sonnet" | "opus" | "deepseek", "reasoning": "<one sentence>" }
Rules: - If task requires external/private data: approach = "rag" - If task requires domain-specific style/behavior and data is stable: approach = "fine_tuning" - Otherwise: approach = "prompt_engineering" - Map complexity (simple/medium/complex) to model tier

section.name: litellm-router-example

list.item: Default to Claude Sonnet 4 for balanced tasks — it's faster and significantly cheaper than Opus with comparable quality for most workloads
Calculate total cost of ownership: API cost + developer fix-time + prompt engineering time — simple models may require more prompt engineering, erasing savings
Route simple classification/extraction to DeepSeek V3 or Haiku; reserve Opus for verifiable reasoning where errors cost real money
Measure before optimizing: deploy with one model, collect traces for 2 weeks, then apply routing logic based on real task distribution
Re-evaluate the framework quarterly — model pricing and capabilities shift dramatically (DeepSeek V3 cut prices ~10x vs GPT-4o equivalents in late 2024)
Latency budgets matter for UX: 10s+ responses (Opus) break user experience in synchronous flows; use async/batch for long-running tasks

section.name: limitations
section.p: Cost comparison tables become stale within weeks as providers change pricing — treat as directional, not absolute. Framework favors quantifiable factors (cost, latency, tokens) over hard-to-measure ones (trust, brand alignment, compliance). "Match model to complexity" advice requires prior knowledge of what "complex" means for your workload — benchmark first. Fine-tuning ROI is often overestimated: 6x inference cost + training cost + maintenance rarely breaks even unless volume is very high (1M+ requests/month). Multi-model routing adds operational complexity: more failure modes, more SDKs to maintain, inconsistent output formatting.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Multi-model routing introduces inconsistent output formats — if one pipeline stage uses Opus and another uses Haiku, the output schema must be enforced via structured output on every model. Model fallback chains must be tested: if Opus is unavailable, the fallback to Sonnet should produce acceptable (not just non-empty) output — test the fallback path explicitly. Human-in-loop checkpoint: before switching a production pipeline from a capable model (Opus) to a cheaper one (DeepSeek), run a side-by-side quality evaluation with a human reviewer on a representative sample. Cost alerts: set a hard budget cap in Helicone/Langfuse to catch runaway agentic loops that call expensive models in a retry loop. Fine-tuning decision is irreversible in the short term — committing to fine-tuning a model means maintaining the dataset and retraining as the base model updates.

template.purpose: Decision record template: requirements, options considered, cost projection, success metrics
LiteLLM complexity-based routing: simple→DeepSeek, balanced→Sonnet, complex→Opus
